subject.title: English B
subject.description:
**IB English B** is a **Group 2** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **language acquisition through five prescribed themes: identities, experiences, human ingenuity, social organization, and sharing the planet**, with a strong focus on **developing receptive, productive, and interactive communication skills in English as a second language**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Productive skills - writing | 1 hour | 1 hour 15 minutes | 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Receptive skills - listening and reading comprehension | 1 hour 45 minutes | 2 hours | 50% |
| **Individual Oral** | Internal assessment - oral communication | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 15 min prep + 10 min exam | 25% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, focused, and directly linked to the analysis of the chosen texts
The topic is communicated accurately and effectively throughout the introduction and body
The line of inquiry remains consistently appropriate to the stated purpose
weaknesses: Methodology is not explicitly articulated or framed within a clear analytical approach
Rationale for secondary source selection is not fully explained
Citations are uneven, with incomplete publication data and inconsistent page numbering
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission communicates the topic and research question with clarity and precision, linking both effectively to the body of the essay. However, the absence of an explicit methodology section or framework and the uneven citation practice prevent a top-of-band score. The work demonstrates strong focus and purpose but lacks full methodological transparency, meriting a score of 5.

strengths: Primary texts are quoted accurately and integrated into the argument
Secondary sources supply relevant historical context that underpins claims
Subject knowledge of both Conan Doyleâs narratives and Victorian society is logically organized
weaknesses: Some generalizations (e.g., on alcoholism) lack sufficient nuance or substantiation
Use of academic conventions (capitalization, consistency in terminology) is uneven
Occasional reliance on single citations without deeper critical engagement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge of the subject, with generally relevant application of source material. However, occasional over-generalizations, uneven academic conventions, and limited depth of source engagement fall short of the highest band. A score of 4 reflects mostly effective comprehension and use of terms but not consistently precise alignment with the 5â6 descriptors.

strengths: Research evidence is consistently relevant to the research question, balancing primary quotations with reputable secondary scholarship
Effective close readings connect textual detail to historical themes (e.g., phrenology, class prejudice)
The conclusion offers suggestions for future research, demonstrating forward-thinking critical engagement
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely thematic and explanatory without deeper interrogation of narrative technique or authorial intent
Evaluation of source limitations and alternative readings is minimal
The argument does not integrate counter-arguments or critical perspectives to address inconsistencies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall, the discussion displays a good to very good quality of critical thinking: research is clearly relevant, and arguments are coherently structured. While there are minor inconsistencies and limited depth in evaluation, the strength of the primary-critical links and forward-looking suggestions justify a high mark within the 7â9 band. A score of 9 recognizes substantial critical engagement while acknowledging areas for deeper analysis and evaluation.

strengths: The structure is clearly appropriate and complete, with a functional table of contents, section headings, and logical sequencing
Pages are numbered correctly, aiding readability
Title page and word count information are present and positioned correctly
weaknesses: Minor typographical inconsistencies in the works-cited formatting
Occasional stray symbols appear, suggesting room for tighter proofreading
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets all key internal assessment requirements: structure is comprehensive, and layout elements (title page, headings, pagination) are correctly applied. Minor formatting lapses do not significantly impair readability. A top presentation score of 4 is appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly phrased on the title page and is narrow enough to be answered within 4 000 words by concentrating on three short stories.  Its wording (âWhat aspects â¦ are reflected â¦?â) allows for focused literary-historical investigation and is directly addressed in the argument.  Minor imprecision remainsâthe question promises a focus on âVictorian Londonâ but class and gender points occasionally broaden to Britain in generalâso a top-of-band but not perfect score is appropriate.

The student offers logical readings of textual details (e.g., Holmesâs deductions from the hat, Watsonâs language toward lower-class characters) and links them to historical commentary.  Analysis, however, remains largely thematic and explanatory; it rarely probes narrative technique or authorial intent, and some assertions are left undeveloped.  Minor inconsistencies and limited depth place it solidly in the middle of the 4â6 range.

Primary texts are quoted accurately and secondary sources (Steinbach, Mack, Wohl, etc.) are used to supply historical context that is largely pertinent to the claims being made.  While integration is generally sound, some statements rely on assertion or single citations without deeper engagement, preventing the consistently âclearâ alignment required for the 5â6 band.

Basic layout conventionsâtitle page, word count, headings, page numbersâare present and mostly correct.  Minor MLA/APA inconsistencies in the works-cited formatting and a few typographical symbols (e.g., stray $ signs, Greek Pi) show some lapses, so âlayout is present and correctly appliedâ in most but not all respects.

The essay demonstrates a secure grasp of both Conan Doyleâs narratives and central features of Victorian society (class hierarchy, separate spheres ideology, phrenology).  Knowledge is logically organised and generally supported by sources, though some generalisations (e.g., alcoholism âconsidered inappropriate for both men and womenâ) lack nuance.  Overall clarity is evident but depth and synthesis fall short of the highest standard.

The essay follows a clear introduction-body-conclusion structure, includes a functional table of contents, section headings and a works-cited list, meeting all EE structural requirements.  Pagination and logical sequencing aid readability; therefore the structure is âclearly appropriate and completeâ.

The selection of evidenceâfrom primary quotations to reputable historical scholarshipâdemonstrates clear relevance to the research question.  Coverage of reputation, class and gender is balanced, but the range of secondary criticism on Conan Doyle is modest and the essay does not interrogate counter-arguments.  Hence the research is âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ.

The essay states a precise focus on âwhat aspects of Victorian London society are reflectedâ in three specified Sherlock Holmes stories.  The student explains the historicalâliterary context in the introduction and links it explicitly to the chosen texts, demonstrating a strong match between topic, subject and category 3 expectations.  Throughout the body, the discussion repeatedly returns to the stated purpose (reputation, class, gender) so that the line of inquiry remains clear and consistently appropriate.

The discussion progresses coherently from image/class to gender, culminating in a conclusion that synthesises main findings.  Nevertheless, evaluation of the sourcesâ limitations or of alternative readings is minimal, and the conclusion largely restates points rather than critically reflecting on them.  Reasoned argument is present but not developed to the level required for 7â9.

The student explains the choice of primary texts and lists a range of secondary historical sources, indicating some informed selection.  However, there is no explicit methodology section dealing with literary-critical approaches, evaluation of source reliability, or criteria for inclusion.  Citations are uneven (e.g., incomplete publication data, inconsistent page numbering).  Consequently the methodology is âmostly completeâ rather than fully explicit or systematic.

Key terms such as âreputationâ, âseparate spheresâ, âaristocracyâ, and âphrenologyâ are correctly introduced and generally used accurately.  Nonetheless, academic conventions (e.g., capitalisation of titles, consistent use of âVictorianâ vs. âvictorianâ) are uneven, and occasional phrasing (âHe became a wrack of a manâ) shows lapses.  The accuracy is therefore âmostlyâ rather than âconsistentlyâ precise.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated, focused, and directly linked to the analysis of the chosen texts, guiding the essay effectively.
The title is prominently stated at the start, meeting the formatting requirement for clear identification of the investigation.
The word count is indicated and falls within the 4000-word limit, satisfying the formatting requirements.
Consider adding a brief overview of the literary-critical approach or methodological framework on the title page to foreground your analytical lens.
The table of contents is complete, structured logically, and meets EE presentation standards.
Adding dot leaders between section titles and page numbers can improve readability and adhere more closely to formal table-of-contents conventions.
Ensure page numbers appear in the footer as visible numerals on every content page to satisfy the formatting criterion.
The introduction effectively uses Steinbach and Cody to contextualize Victorian social classes, demonstrating clear subject knowledge.
The introduction provides context but lacks explicit articulation of the research methodology; include a concise methods overview here.
In-text citations vary in form (e.g., year placement); standardize to a single style throughout.
Explicitly justify the selection of the three stories by stating your criteria in the introduction.
The summary of The Blue Carbuncle is accurate and concise, demonstrating clear understanding of the primary text.
There is a stray Greek Pi symbol in the section header, interrupting the formal presentationâremove these artifacts.
The narrative summaries extend into lengthy retellings; consider condensing these to focus more on your analytical claims.
Your rationale for secondary source selection is not articulated; add a sentence explaining your criteria for inclusion.
Including the publication year for each Doyle story is good practice, showing attention to source details.
Good contextualization of phrenology with Parssinen (1974) deepens the thematic analysis of Holmesâs deductions.
The citation âpp. <math>2-3</math>â appears in math mode; convert page ranges to plain text for consistency.
Clarify the shift from descriptive summary to critical argument in this section to strengthen analytical focus.
Comparative reading of Ryder and St. Clair effectively highlights the social pressures of reputation in Victorian society.
Assertions about class distinctions would benefit from nuanced discussion or reference to contrary examples.
Consider integrating recent scholarship on class fluidity to deepen the analysis of social mobility themes.
Effective close reading of âhorsey-looking manâ illustrates class prejudice; this detail strengthens your argument.
Provide context for the Â£1,000 reward by explaining its relative value or purchasing power in Victorian London.
The statement on alcoholism lacks specific supporting detail; cite a primary or secondary source to substantiate this view.
Your thematic link between addiction and reputation insightfully connects personal vice to societal values.
The Darwin citation adds depth to gender analysis; ensure full bibliographic details appear in your works cited.
The assertion on the purpose of housekeeping requires broader historiographical evidence; integrate an additional scholarly viewpoint.
Your conclusion summarizes key themes well, but it would benefit from reflection on methodological limitations.
Consider adding a brief reflection on your research process and decision-making to demonstrate engagement and growth.
The suggestion for future research effectively extends the scope, indicating critical thinking about the studyâs continuation.
The works-cited list shows thorough research, but entries should follow a consistent citation style (italicization, punctuation).
Secondary source entries are not alphabetized by author; reorder to align with formal bibliography conventions.
Consider integrating peer-reviewed journal articles on Victorian literature to broaden the scholarly depth of your research.
